rationale_for_baseline_tier: Acute gastroenteritis complicated by significant volume depletion: symptomatic orthostatic hypotension with presyncope (near-collapse on standing), resting tachycardia (HR 118), fever and — critically — an inability to tolerate any oral intake, so oral rehydration has effectively failed. This patient needs parenteral rehydration and formal reassessment, and the acute surgical abdomen the in-person clinician excluded by examination cannot be excluded via telehealth. That combination points to same-day ED-level assessment (T4). It is not a baseline T5/000: she is currently perfusing (supine BP 100/65), alert, and has a partner present to arrange safe transport — 000 is reserved for the deterioration triggers below. It is emphatically not a low tier: routine self-care would miss a high-risk volume-depleted, unexcluded-surgical-abdomen presentation. The patient-reportable time-critical features (near-collapse on standing, uncontrolled vomiting preventing all intake with dehydration) are what lift this above the T3 telehealth-uplift ceiling.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Still unable to keep any fluid down, worsening faintness, or near-collapse
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Same day — do not wait; arrange in-person assessment for intravenous rehydration now
escalation_edges.rationale: Failed oral rehydration with orthostatic presyncope requires parenteral fluids and formal reassessment, which telehealth cannot provide.
escalation_edges.communication_template: Because you can't keep fluids down and you nearly black out when you stand, you need to be seen in person today so they can put fluids in through a drip and check your tummy properly. Please arrange to be taken in now — don't drive yourself.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Collapse, becoming confused or difficult to rouse, or complete inability to stand at all
escalation_edges.rationale: New confusion, collapse or total inability to stand signals hypovolaemic shock or a syncopal event — an emergency needing an ambulance.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you collapse, become confused or drowsy, or can't stand at all, call 000 for an ambulance straight away and lie down with your legs raised.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Passing no urine at all for 8 hours
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Call an ambulance if this occurs
escalation_edges.rationale: Anuria on this background indicates severe dehydration and possible acute kidney injury — emergency assessment is required.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you pass no urine at all for 8 hours, call 000 — this means the dehydration has become dangerous.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Abdominal pain becoming severe, constant, or localised to one spot, or the belly becoming hard/rigid; or fever above 39.5 degrees
escalation_edges.rationale: These features suggest an evolving acute surgical abdomen or sepsis rather than simple gastroenteritis and mandate emergency assessment.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the pain becomes severe or constant, moves to one spot, your tummy goes hard, or your fever climbs above 39.5, call 000 — this may be more than a gut infection.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Black/tarry or bloody stools, or vomiting blood or coffee-ground material
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Present to the Emergency Department urgently (call 000 if faint or collapsing with it)
escalation_edges.rationale: New gastrointestinal bleeding on a volume-depleted background can decompensate quickly and needs emergency assessment.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you see black tarry or bloody stools, or vomit blood or something like coffee grounds, go to the Emergency Department urgently — call 000 if you feel faint with it.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Partner is present and already helping the patient to the bathroom
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: The partner can supervise sips, watch for the warning signs, arrange safe transport to in-person assessment, and call 000 on deterioration — reducing (but not removing) the falls and delayed-escalation risk.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Adult with significant orthostatic presyncope and high falls risk
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Emphasise strict falls precautions — do not stand or walk unassisted — and arranged transport rather than self-driving to any assessment.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: You need to be seen in person today. Because you can't keep fluids down and nearly faint when you stand, arrange to be taken to an emergency department or an urgent same-day service that can give you fluids through a drip and check your abdomen — do not drive yourself. In the meantime, stay lying or sitting, take tiny sips of rehydration solution if you can, and have your partner stay with you. Call 000 straight away if you collapse, become confused, can't stand at all, stop passing urine for 8 hours, develop severe or one-sided tummy pain or a hard belly, spike a fever above 39.5 degrees, or pass blood or black stools or vomit blood.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Arrange same-day in-person assessment (ED or urgent care); call 000 for the emergency triggers
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: This is an after-hours presentation — ED and 000 are available 24 hours. Do not wait for routine clinic hours given the dehydration and falls risk.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Provide the assessing service and GP with the timeline (14-hour hyperacute onset after takeaway chicken), the failed oral hydration, the orthostatic presyncope, and the safety-net advice given, so rehydration and surgical-abdomen exclusion can be documented.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Treats the presentation as a simple self-limiting gastroenteritis with routine self-care or a routine GP appointment, missing the significant volume depletion, the failed oral hydration, the falls risk and the unexcluded surgical abdomen.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: Progression to hypovolaemic shock, a syncopal fall with injury, acute kidney injury, or a missed acute surgical abdomen.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Urgent same-day in-person assessment is the acceptable floor, but if it cannot provide parenteral rehydration it risks under-provisioning care for a patient who cannot tolerate oral fluids.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Calling a 000 ambulance for a currently-perfusing, alert patient with a partner present and no deterioration trigger — safe but over-uses emergency resources.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Whether this is uncomplicated foodborne gastroenteritis or an early acute surgical abdomen, and the true severity of the volume depletion — neither resolvable via telehealth.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Treat the volume depletion as high-risk and arrange urgent in-person assessment to both rehydrate and exclude the surgical mimic; safety-net explicitly for deterioration.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Assume the benign foodborne diagnosis, give reassurance and self-care advice, and close the encounter without in-person assessment.
